findings.title: Database connections not configured for TLS encryption
findings.description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (subtask 1007) defines CNPG_SIGMA1_URL and VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL without TLS parameters (e.g., sslmode=require for PostgreSQL, rediss:// for Valkey). All five backend services connect to PostgreSQL and Valkey over unencrypted channels within the cluster. While in-cluster traffic may be considered lower risk, a compromised pod or network-level attacker performing ARP spoofing could intercept credentials and sensitive financial/PII data in transit between application pods and databases.
findings.recommendation: Update CNPG_SIGMA1_URL to include ?sslmode=require (or verify-full with CA cert mounted). Enable TLS on the CNPG Cluster CR via spec.postgresql.pg_hba entries requiring SSL. For Valkey, enable TLS via the operator CR and switch to rediss:// scheme. Mount CA certificates as projected volumes in application pods.

findings.title: GDPR internal endpoints rely on weak defense-in-depth headers instead of strong authentication
findings.description: The GDPR export and delete endpoints across all five services use different ad-hoc authentication mechanisms: equipment-catalog checks X-Internal-Request header (subtask 2010), RMS checks X-Internal-Key against an env var (subtask 3011), finance checks X-Internal-Key (subtask 4012), and customer-vetting has no specific auth mentioned beyond JWT (subtask 5007). This inconsistency means some services have weaker protection on endpoints capable of mass data deletion. The GDPR delete operation is irreversible and should have the strongest authentication.
findings.recommendation: Standardize internal endpoint authentication using mutual TLS (mTLS) between pods via Cilium or a service mesh, or use a single shared HMAC-signed internal token validated identically across all services. Additionally, require a double-confirmation mechanism for GDPR delete operations — e.g., a time-limited deletion token that must be generated first, then submitted with the delete request.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET stored as a single static Kubernetes Secret with no rotation mechanism
findings.description: The JWT_SECRET (subtask 1005) is a single symmetric key shared across all services for JWT validation. There is no key rotation strategy, no key versioning (kid header), and no JWKS endpoint. If this key is compromised, all service authentication is bypassed. The secret rotation CronJob (subtask 10012) only logs a reminder — it does not actually rotate secrets.
findings.recommendation: Implement asymmetric JWT signing (RS256 or ES256) with a JWKS endpoint so that services only need the public key for validation. Support key rotation by including a kid (key ID) claim in tokens and serving multiple keys from the JWKS endpoint. At minimum, support dual-key validation (current + previous) to allow zero-downtime rotation.

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature validation implementation details are underspecified
findings.description: The Stripe webhook endpoint (subtask 4005) mentions validating the Stripe-Signature header but the implementation details mention 'stripe-rust verify_webhook or manual HMAC-SHA256 computation with reqwest.' The reqwest mention is concerning — reqwest is an HTTP client, not a crypto library. Incorrect webhook signature validation is a common vulnerability that can lead to forged payment events, causing the system to mark invoices as paid without actual payment.
findings.recommendation: Use the official stripe-rust SDK's Webhook::construct_event() function exclusively — do not implement manual HMAC validation. Ensure the raw request body bytes are captured before any deserialization (the plan correctly notes this). Add replay protection by checking the timestamp tolerance (reject events older than 5 minutes). Write a dedicated negative test with a tampered signature to verify rejection.

findings.title: Signal-CLI uses :latest tag with no image pinning or scanning
findings.description: The Signal-CLI deployment (subtask 1006) uses image signalapp/signal-cli:latest, which is mutable and could be replaced with a compromised image at any time. There is no mention of container image scanning for any service images. The Signal-CLI pod has access to the Signal registration data PVC containing cryptographic identity keys.
findings.recommendation: Pin all container images to specific SHA256 digests (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli@sha256:abc...). Implement container image scanning in CI using Trivy or Grype as part of the security.yml workflow (subtask 10015). Add an admission controller (Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) to block deployment of images with CRITICAL vulnerabilities or :latest tags.

findings.title: No RBAC/permissions model defined for multi-user access within services
findings.description: The JWT middleware implementations across services (subtasks 2003, 4010, 5007) only distinguish between 'admin' and non-admin roles. There is no tenant isolation, organization-scoped access control, or fine-grained permission model. For example, any authenticated user can view any other organization's invoices via GET /api/v1/invoices — there are no org_id authorization checks in query handlers.
findings.recommendation: Implement organization-scoped authorization: extract org_id from JWT claims, inject it into all database queries as a mandatory WHERE clause (e.g., WHERE org_id = $claims.org_id). Create a middleware or query builder abstraction that enforces this automatically. Define at minimum three roles: admin, org_owner, and org_member with different permission sets.

findings.title: SQL injection risk in dynamic query construction for catalog product search
findings.description: The equipment catalog product search handler (subtask 2004) mentions 'Build dynamic sqlx query using QueryBuilder' with ILIKE clauses for user-provided search strings. While sqlx parameterizes queries, the dynamic QueryBuilder pattern requires careful use to avoid concatenating user input directly into SQL fragments, especially with ILIKE operators that accept pattern characters (%, _).
findings.recommendation: Ensure all user-supplied values are passed exclusively through sqlx bind parameters ($1, $2, etc.), never interpolated into SQL strings. For ILIKE searches, escape special SQL pattern characters (%, _, \) in the search string before binding. Add integration tests that pass SQL injection payloads (e.g., search='; DROP TABLE products;--) and verify they return empty results, not errors.

findings.title: PII stored in multiple schemas without encryption at rest or field-level encryption
findings.description: Customer PII (names, emails, phone numbers, addresses) is stored across multiple schemas: crew_members (name, email, phone), opportunities (customer_id linked to PII), invoices (org_id linked to PII), and vetting_results (opencorporates_data JSONB containing business owner names, addresses). None of the migration subtasks mention column-level encryption, and there is no mention of PostgreSQL TDE or pgcrypto usage for sensitive fields despite pgcrypto being installed.
findings.recommendation: Identify and classify all PII fields across schemas. Use pgcrypto to encrypt sensitive fields at rest (e.g., crew_members.email, crew_members.phone). Alternatively, implement application-level envelope encryption using a KMS. Document the data classification in a data protection register and ensure the GDPR export endpoints decrypt data for export while the delete endpoints properly purge encrypted fields.

findings.title: ConfigMap contains database connection URL with username in plaintext
findings.description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (subtask 1007) stores CNPG_SIGMA1_URL containing the database username (sigma1_user) in plaintext. While the password is noted as being injected separately, the username exposure in a ConfigMap (which is not encrypted at rest by default in Kubernetes) reduces the attacker's required information to compromise the database to just the password.
findings.recommendation: Move the full database connection URL (including username) into a Secret rather than a ConfigMap. ConfigMaps are stored unencrypted in etcd by default and are visible to anyone with read access to the namespace. Alternatively, enable etcd encryption at rest for both ConfigMaps and Secrets.

findings.title: Equipment API endpoints lack authentication for AI agent access
findings.description: The equipment API endpoints (subtask 2007) — GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog and POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout — are described as 'machine-readable JSON for AI agents' but have no specific authentication requirements mentioned. The checkout endpoint creates availability blocks (effectively booking inventory) and should not be publicly accessible without authentication.
findings.recommendation: Require API key or JWT authentication on all /api/v1/equipment-api/* endpoints. For AI agent access, issue service-specific API keys with rate limits lower than general user limits. The checkout endpoint should additionally require a valid JWT with a specific 'agent' or 'service' role claim.

findings.title: Audit trail for GDPR operations uses inconsistent schemas across services
findings.description: GDPR delete operations log to different locations: RMS logs to audit.gdpr_deletions (subtask 3011), finance logs to finance.gdpr_deletions (subtask 4012), customer-vetting logs to audit.deletion_log (subtask 5007), and equipment-catalog does not mention audit logging for GDPR deletes (subtask 2010). There is no unified audit trail, making compliance verification difficult.
findings.recommendation: Create a centralized audit.gdpr_operations table in the audit schema with a consistent structure: (id UUID, operation TEXT, service TEXT, customer_id UUID, operator TEXT, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, details JSONB). All GDPR operations across all services should write to this single table. Add the equipment-catalog service's GDPR delete to the audit logging.

findings.title: No Pod Security Standards enforcement configured
findings.description: There is no mention of PodSecurityStandards (PSS), SecurityContext settings, or PodSecurityAdmission policies across any service deployment manifests. Without these, pods may run as root, have writable root filesystems, or gain unnecessary Linux capabilities that increase the attack surface if a container is compromised.
findings.recommendation: Set SecurityContext on all pod specs: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ['ALL']. Enable PodSecurity admission controller at the namespace level with 'restricted' profile for sigma1 and databases namespaces. Add a tmpfs volume mount for /tmp where services need writable temporary storage.

findings.title: No dependency auditing task in CI/CD pipeline
findings.description: While the security.yml workflow (subtask 10015 area) mentions Semgrep, cargo-audit, nancy, and npm audit, the actual subtask 10015 has empty details and is the last subtask with minimal specification. Dependency auditing is critical for detecting known CVEs in the supply chain. The Rust services pin versions in Cargo.toml but the Node.js and Go services do not have lockfile verification specified.
findings.recommendation: Fully specify the security.yml workflow with concrete steps: cargo-audit for Rust services, nancy or govulncheck for Go, npm audit --audit-level=high for Node.js. Run dependency audits on every PR and nightly on main. Pin all dependency versions and commit lockfiles (Cargo.lock, go.sum, package-lock.json/bun.lockb). Add a Renovate or Dependabot configuration for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: Web chat CORS configured as permissive wildcard
findings.description: The Morgan web chat adapter (subtask 7007) sets cors_origins: ['*'], and the equipment-catalog tower-http CorsLayer (subtask 2002) is described as 'permissive for dev'. If these permissive CORS configurations are not tightened for production, they allow any origin to make authenticated API requests, potentially enabling CSRF-like attacks.
findings.recommendation: Create environment-specific CORS configurations. For production, restrict cors_origins to the exact Sigma-1 domain(s): ['https://sigma1.com', 'https://chat.sigma1.com']. Use Helm values to parameterize CORS origins so they differ between dev and production deployments.

findings.title: OpenAI API receives customer event photos for AI curation scoring
findings.description: The social media curation pipeline (subtask 6005) sends customer event photos to OpenAI's gpt-4o vision API for scoring. This means potentially sensitive event photos (which could contain identifiable individuals) are transmitted to a third-party AI provider. This has GDPR and privacy implications if photos contain personal data of event attendees who have not consented to AI processing.
findings.recommendation: Add a data processing notice in the social upload flow informing users that photos will be processed by AI. Review OpenAI's data processing agreement for GDPR compliance. Consider implementing a local pre-screening step (e.g., face detection to flag photos with identifiable individuals) before sending to external AI. Ensure the GDPR export includes records of which photos were sent to AI providers.

findings.title: Signal-CLI manual registration step creates a security gap
findings.description: Signal-CLI requires manual registration (subtask 1006) including running verification commands inside the pod. The phone number registration state is stored on a PVC, but there is no encryption on this PVC. If the PVC is accessed by an attacker, they could impersonate Morgan's Signal identity.
findings.recommendation: Enable encryption at rest for the signal-cli-data PVC using a StorageClass with encryption enabled (most cloud providers support this). Restrict PVC access to only the signal-cli pod using a dedicated ServiceAccount and RBAC. Document the registration process as a security-sensitive operation in the ops runbook.

findings.title: File upload endpoints lack comprehensive validation and sanitization
findings.description: The social media upload endpoint (subtask 6004) accepts multipart file uploads but only mentions validating content-type is multipart/form-data. There is no mention of file size limits, file type validation (magic bytes, not just extension), filename sanitization, or malware scanning. Uploaded files are stored directly to R2 and later processed by sharp, which has had CVEs related to malicious image processing.
findings.recommendation: Implement: (1) Maximum file size limit per file (e.g., 50MB) and per request (e.g., 200MB). (2) Validate file type using magic bytes (file signature), not just Content-Type header or extension. (3) Sanitize filenames to prevent path traversal. (4) Process uploaded images through sharp with try/catch to handle malformed images gracefully. (5) Consider running ClamAV or similar scanning on uploads before R2 storage. (6) Set a Content-Disposition header on R2 objects to prevent direct browser execution.

findings.title: No security event logging or anomaly detection configured
findings.description: While Prometheus metrics and health endpoints are implemented across all services, there is no security-specific event logging: failed authentication attempts, GDPR operations, admin actions, rate limit triggers, or suspicious patterns are not explicitly logged to a searchable security audit stream. The Loki integration mentioned in production hardening focuses on operational alerts, not security events.
findings.recommendation: Create a structured security event log stream using tracing/pino with a dedicated 'security' log level or tag. Log: all failed JWT validations with source IP, all rate limit triggers with client identifier, all GDPR export/delete requests with operator identity, all admin write operations, and all Stripe webhook validation failures. Route security logs to a dedicated Loki stream with longer retention (1 year minimum for GDPR compliance). Create Loki alert rules for anomalous patterns (e.g., >10 failed auth attempts from same IP in 1 minute).

strengths: CiliumNetworkPolicies are comprehensively planned for all services with explicit ingress/egress rules, implementing a zero-trust network model (subtasks 10004-10009)
Per-service PostgreSQL roles with schema-scoped USAGE grants (subtask 1003) implement proper database-level access control and least privilege
GDPR data export and deletion endpoints are implemented across all five backend services, demonstrating privacy-by-design thinking
Rate limiting is implemented at the application level (subtask 2006) using sliding window counters in Valkey, providing protection against API abuse
Stripe webhook signature validation is explicitly called out as a requirement (subtask 4005), showing awareness of payment security concerns
The Cloudflare Tunnel approach (subtask 10010) avoids exposing any services via NodePort or LoadBalancer, significantly reducing the external attack surface
The plan includes a default-deny CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy (scale task 15) as the baseline, which is the correct zero-trust starting point
Background tasks for vetting and social media use proper timeout wrapping (10s per step) preventing resource exhaustion from hanging external API calls
The RBAC subtask (10011) explicitly disables automountServiceAccountToken and creates per-service ServiceAccounts with least-privilege roles
summary: The Sigma-1 platform plan demonstrates strong network-level security design with CiliumNetworkPolicies and Cloudflare Tunnel, but has significant gaps in data protection (unencrypted database connections, no field-level PII encryption), authentication consistency (inconsistent GDPR endpoint auth, single static JWT secret), and input validation (file upload sanitization, dynamic SQL construction). The most critical issues to address before production are: enabling TLS on database connections, standardizing internal endpoint authentication with mTLS, implementing proper file upload validation, and establishing a unified GDPR audit trail. The 18 findings span 3 high, 12 medium, and 3 low severity issues requiring remediation during implementation.
